turns.student: Hi, I'm here to help you. What's been going on?
turns.patient: I've had this crampy pain in my lower left abdomen for weeks now, and it's getting worse. It's really affecting my work - I'm a software engineer, and I have trouble focusing when I'm running to the bathroom every 10 minutes with loose stools.

turns.student: That sounds really uncomfortable. I can tell this has been weighing on you.
turns.patient: It is, it's like my body just won't cooperate sometimes. And yeah, it's not just the pain and diarrhea, but also the fatigue - I'm working 60+ hour weeks and I feel like I'm running on fumes most of the time.

turns.student: I want to make sure we don't miss anything important. When these episodes happen, does your body react in any other way?
turns.patient: It just feels like my whole lower abdomen tightens up and hurts, but no other symptoms really - no fever or chills, no nausea, nothing like that. Just this crampy pain and the diarrhea.

turns.student: I know it can be scary to think about, but do you notice any sweating or feeling faint during these episodes?
turns.patient: No, I don't really sweat much when I have an episode, and I've never felt like passing out. But I do feel a bit dizzy for a few seconds after rushing to the bathroom, mainly because of the urgency with my bowel movements.

turns.student: Thank you for sharing that. I really appreciate your honesty. Does the discomfort stay in one place or does it spread anywhere?
turns.patient: It usually stays around the left lower abdomen, but sometimes it feels like it's radiating down to my groin area on the left side.

turns.student: It must be frightening when symptoms like these happen. Any other areas of your body affected?
turns.patient: Only my stomach and lower abdomen are really affected by this, but like I mentioned earlier, it's been impacting my work and daily life in a big way. The fatigue is probably the worst part - it's hard to focus on anything when you're feeling exhausted all the time.

turns.student: I'm concerned about you. Have you noticed any swelling anywhere? Even minor changes?
turns.patient: To be honest, I haven't really paid attention to swelling in my body, but I do notice that my underwear gets a bit more soiled than usual because of the urgency and frequency of my bowel movements. That's about it, nothing else that comes to mind.

turns.student: Sometimes when people are going through something like this, they also feel nauseous or have stomach upset. Has that happened to you?
turns.patient: No, I don't really get nausea with these episodes. Like I said, it's mostly just the crampy pain and diarrhea for me.

turns.student: I can see this is taking a toll on you. How has your energy been? Your appetite?
turns.patient: My energy's been really low, to be honest. The fatigue is one of the worst parts - I'm usually someone who's very productive and can handle long work hours, but lately, I've just been exhausted all the time. And my appetite... well, it's not great either. I've been eating mostly takeout because I don't have the energy to cook, and even that doesn't seem to be giving me much satisfaction anymore.

turns.student: Have you had any fevers or night sweats? I know it might seem unrelated, but it helps me understand the full picture.
turns.patient: I have been experiencing a low-grade fever, usually around 37.8 to 38.0 degrees Celsius, just a few times per week. It's not every day, but often enough that I've noticed it. And actually, it might be worth mentioning that I've also been waking up multiple times at night because of the diarrhea, which has been really disrupting my sleep.

turns.student: Your health really matters to me as your care provider. Can you tell me about any other medical conditions you've been diagnosed with?
turns.patient: I had an appendectomy when I was 16, that was about 20 years ago now, and everything seemed fine after that. But aside from that, nothing else comes to mind - no chronic illnesses or ongoing health issues.

turns.student: I know medications can be a hassle. Are you taking everything as prescribed, or has anything been hard to keep up with?
turns.patient: To be honest, I haven't been following any prescription medication regimen for this issue. In fact, I've been self-medicating with loperamide (Imodium) for diarrhea over the past two weeks, but it hasn't really provided much relief - if anything, it's just masked the symptoms temporarily.

turns.student: I don't want to pry, but understanding your lifestyle really helps me help you. How do you eat on a typical day?
turns.patient: Honestly, with my schedule and energy levels, I've been eating pretty irregularly lately. Mostly just takeout or leftovers because cooking takes too much effort. I'm not really keeping track of what I eat from one meal to the next - it's whatever's convenient at the time.

turns.student: It sounds like your life keeps you really busy. Do you get a chance to exercise or stay active?
turns.patient: To be honest, with my work schedule and the way I've been feeling lately, exercise has fallen by the wayside. I used to try to go for runs or bike rides on weekends, but even those have become too much of an effort with the fatigue and other symptoms. It's hard to find time for anything outside of work right now.

turns.student: I hate to ask this, but it's really important — has anyone in your family had serious health problems?
turns.patient: My older sister was diagnosed with Crohn's disease when she was 30 years old. That was a few years ago, and it's been pretty tough on our whole family. She's been dealing with some of the same symptoms I'm experiencing now, like chronic diarrhea and abdominal pain, so that's definitely something that's been on my mind lately.

turns.student: I'm so sorry to hear that. That must have been incredibly difficult. Do you worry about your own health because of it?
turns.patient: Yeah, I do worry about my own health now, especially since I've started experiencing similar symptoms. It's hard not to think about the possibility of something like Crohn's or another serious condition being the cause of all this discomfort and fatigue. But at the same time, it's also a bit reassuring that there might be a diagnosis for what I'm going through, which is why I'm trying to be as open and honest with you about everything.

turns.student: Given everything you've been through, I want to be thorough. Do you drink alcohol? How do you cope with stress?
turns.patient: Yeah, I do drink alcohol. Typically 2-3 beers on weeknights, but over the past month, I've noticed myself increasing that to 4-5 beers per night because of the stress from my symptoms and work. It's become a way for me to unwind, but I know it's not healthy.

turns.student: I want to make sure we're being thorough. Do you smoke or use any substances?
turns.patient: No, I don't smoke. And actually, aside from drinking more beer than usual lately because of stress and symptoms, I'm not really using any other substances.

turns.student: Is there anything you've been hesitant to tell me? I promise there's no judgment here.
turns.patient: Honestly, I think we've covered a lot of ground already. But if I had to say one thing, it's that I'm worried about what this could mean for my career and my life in general. As a software engineer, I'm used to being very productive and managing multiple projects at once, but with these symptoms, I feel like I'm barely holding on. It's hard not to worry about how this will affect my work performance and whether I'll be able to keep up with the demands of my job.

turns.student: Before we wrap up, is there anything else you're worried about that you want to share?
turns.patient: To be honest, one thing that keeps me up at night is wondering if this could be related to something more serious, like Crohn's disease or another chronic condition. My sister's diagnosis was a big wake-up call for me, and I'm scared about what this might mean for my future health and well-being. But also, on a practical level, I'm worried about how this will affect my daily life and career - it's already impacting my work performance, and if things don't get better soon, I'll have to figure out how to manage everything while dealing with these symptoms.
